question:
A-2. Show that, for any positive integer \( n \),
\[
\sum_{r=0}^{[(n-1) / 2]}\left\{\frac{n-2 r}{n}\binom{n}{r}\right\}^{2}=\frac{1}{n}\binom{2 n-2}{n-1}
\]
where \( [x] \) means the greatest integer not exceeding \( x \), and \( \left({ }_{r}^{n}\right) \) is the binomial coefficient
solution:
A-2. Substituting \( s=n-r \) in the given summation reveals that twice this sum is equal to:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{r=0}^{n}\left\{\frac{n-2 r}{n}\binom{n}{r}\right\}^{2} \\
=\sum\left(1-2 \frac{r}{n}\right)^{2}\binom{n}{r}^{2}=\sum\binom{n}{r}^{2}-4 \sum \frac{r}{n}\binom{n}{r}\binom{n}{r}+4 \sum\left(\frac{r}{n}\right)^{2}\binom{n}{r}^{2} \\
=\binom{2 n}{n}-4 \sum\binom{n-1}{r-1}\binom{n}{r}+4 \sum\binom{n-1}{r-1}^{2} \\
=\binom{2 n}{n}-4\binom{2 n-1}{n-1}+4\binom{2 n-2}{n-1}=\binom{2 n}{n}-4\binom{2 n-2}{n-2} \\
=\left\{\frac{2 n(2 n-1)}{n^{2}}-4 \frac{n-1}{n}\right\}\binom{2 n-2}{n-1}=\frac{2}{n}\binom{2 n-2}{n-1}
\end{array}
\]

Comment: This solution assumes the well-known identities
\[
\sum\binom{n}{r}^{2}=\binom{2 n}{n} \text { and } \sum_{r=0}^{k}\binom{m}{k-r}\binom{n}{r}=\binom{m+n}{k}
\]
which may be proved by comparing coefficients in the expansion of
\[
(1+x)^{m} \cdot(1+x)^{n}=(1+x)^{m+n} .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
A-2. Show that, for any positive integer \( intsize \),
\[
\sum_{indexvar=0}^{[(intsize-1) / 2]}\left\{\frac{intsize-2 indexvar}{intsize}\binom{intsize}{indexvar}\right\}^{2}=\frac{1}{intsize}\binom{2 intsize-2}{intsize-1}
\]
where \( [auxvar] \) means the greatest integer not exceeding \( auxvar \), and \( \left({ }_{indexvar}^{intsize}\right) \) is the binomial coefficient
descriptive_long.solution:
A-2. Substituting \( complement=intsize-indexvar \) in the given summation reveals that twice this sum is equal to:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{indexvar=0}^{intsize}\left\{\frac{intsize-2 indexvar}{intsize}\binom{intsize}{indexvar}\right\}^{2} \\
=\sum\left(1-2 \frac{indexvar}{intsize}\right)^{2}\binom{intsize}{indexvar}^{2}=\sum\binom{intsize}{indexvar}^{2}-4 \sum \frac{indexvar}{intsize}\binom{intsize}{indexvar}\binom{intsize}{indexvar}+4 \sum\left(\frac{indexvar}{intsize}\right)^{2}\binom{intsize}{indexvar}^{2} \\
=\binom{2 intsize}{intsize}-4 \sum\binom{intsize-1}{indexvar-1}\binom{intsize}{indexvar}+4 \sum\binom{intsize-1}{indexvar-1}^{2} \\
=\binom{2 intsize}{intsize}-4\binom{2 intsize-1}{intsize-1}+4\binom{2 intsize-2}{intsize-1}=\binom{2 intsize}{intsize}-4\binom{2 intsize-2}{intsize-2} \\
=\left\{\frac{2 intsize(2 intsize-1)}{intsize^{2}}-4 \frac{intsize-1}{intsize}\right\}\binom{2 intsize-2}{intsize-1}=\frac{2}{intsize}\binom{2 intsize-2}{intsize-1}
\end{array}
\]

Comment: This solution assumes the well-known identities
\[
\sum\binom{intsize}{indexvar}^{2}=\binom{2 intsize}{intsize} \text { and } \sum_{indexvar=0}^{summstep}\binom{totalsize}{summstep-indexvar}\binom{intsize}{indexvar}=\binom{totalsize+intsize}{summstep}
\]
which may be proved by comparing coefficients in the expansion of
\[
(1+auxvar)^{totalsize} \cdot(1+auxvar)^{intsize}=(1+auxvar)^{totalsize+intsize} .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem:
<<<
A-2. Show that, for any positive integer \( butterfly \),
\[
\sum_{pineapple=0}^{[(butterfly-1) / 2]}\left\{\frac{butterfly-2 pineapple}{butterfly}\binom{butterfly}{pineapple}\right\}^{2}=\frac{1}{butterfly}\binom{2 butterfly-2}{butterfly-1}
\]
where \( [lighthouse] \) means the greatest integer not exceeding \( lighthouse \), and \( \left({ }_{pineapple}^{butterfly}\right) \) is the binomial coefficient
>>>
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution:
<<<
A-2. Substituting \( waterfall=butterfly-pineapple \) in the given summation reveals that twice this sum is equal to:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{pineapple=0}^{butterfly}\left\{\frac{butterfly-2 pineapple}{butterfly}\binom{butterfly}{pineapple}\right\}^{2} \\
=\sum\left(1-2 \frac{pineapple}{butterfly}\right)^{2}\binom{butterfly}{pineapple}^{2}=\sum\binom{butterfly}{pineapple}^{2}-4 \sum \frac{pineapple}{butterfly}\binom{butterfly}{pineapple}\binom{butterfly}{pineapple}+4 \sum\left(\frac{pineapple}{butterfly}\right)^{2}\binom{butterfly}{pineapple}^{2} \\
=\binom{2 butterfly}{butterfly}-4 \sum\binom{butterfly-1}{pineapple-1}\binom{butterfly}{pineapple}+4 \sum\binom{butterfly-1}{pineapple-1}^{2} \\
=\binom{2 butterfly}{butterfly}-4\binom{2 butterfly-1}{butterfly-1}+4\binom{2 butterfly-2}{butterfly-1}=\binom{2 butterfly}{butterfly}-4\binom{2 butterfly-2}{butterfly-2} \\
=\left\{\frac{2 butterfly(2 butterfly-1)}{butterfly^{2}}-4 \frac{butterfly-1}{butterfly}\right\}\binom{2 butterfly-2}{butterfly-1}=\frac{2}{butterfly}\binom{2 butterfly-2}{butterfly-1}
\end{array}
\]

Comment: This solution assumes the well-known identities
\[
\sum\binom{butterfly}{pineapple}^{2}=\binom{2 butterfly}{butterfly} \text { and } \sum_{pineapple=0}^{saxophone}\binom{asteroid}{saxophone-pineapple}\binom{butterfly}{pineapple}=\binom{asteroid+butterfly}{saxophone}
\]
which may be proved by comparing coefficients in the expansion of
\[
(1+lighthouse)^{asteroid} \cdot(1+lighthouse)^{butterfly}=(1+lighthouse)^{asteroid+butterfly} .
\]
>>>

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
A-2. Show that, for any positive integer \( zeroamount \),
\[
\sum_{motionless=0}^{[(zeroamount-1) / 2]}\left\{\frac{zeroamount-2 motionless}{zeroamount}\binom{zeroamount}{motionless}\right\}^{2}=\frac{1}{zeroamount}\binom{2 zeroamount-2}{zeroamount-1}
\]
where \( [x] \) means the greatest integer not exceeding \( x \), and \( \left({ }_{motionless}^{zeroamount}\right) \) is the binomial coefficient
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-2. Substituting \( augmentation=zeroamount-motionless \) in the given summation reveals that twice this sum is equal to:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{motionless=0}^{zeroamount}\left\{\frac{zeroamount-2 motionless}{zeroamount}\binom{zeroamount}{motionless}\right\}^{2} \\
=\sum\left(1-2 \frac{motionless}{zeroamount}\right)^{2}\binom{zeroamount}{motionless}^{2}=\sum\binom{zeroamount}{motionless}^{2}-4 \sum \frac{motionless}{zeroamount}\binom{zeroamount}{motionless}\binom{zeroamount}{motionless}+4 \sum\left(\frac{motionless}{zeroamount}\right)^{2}\binom{zeroamount}{motionless}^{2} \\
=\binom{2 zeroamount}{zeroamount}-4 \sum\binom{zeroamount-1}{motionless-1}\binom{zeroamount}{motionless}+4 \sum\binom{zeroamount-1}{motionless-1}^{2} \\
=\binom{2 zeroamount}{zeroamount}-4\binom{2 zeroamount-1}{zeroamount-1}+4\binom{2 zeroamount-2}{zeroamount-1}=\binom{2 zeroamount}{zeroamount}-4\binom{2 zeroamount-2}{zeroamount-2} \\
=\left\{\frac{2 zeroamount(2 zeroamount-1)}{zeroamount^{2}}-4 \frac{zeroamount-1}{zeroamount}\right\}\binom{2 zeroamount-2}{zeroamount-1}=\frac{2}{zeroamount}\binom{2 zeroamount-2}{zeroamount-1}
\end{array}
\]

Comment: This solution assumes the well-known identities
\[
\sum\binom{zeroamount}{motionless}^{2}=\binom{2 zeroamount}{zeroamount} \text { and } \sum_{motionless=0}^{knownness}\binom{emptiness}{knownness-motionless}\binom{zeroamount}{motionless}=\binom{emptiness+zeroamount}{knownness}
\]
which may be proved by comparing coefficients in the expansion of
\[
(1+invariable)^{emptiness} \cdot(1+invariable)^{zeroamount}=(1+invariable)^{emptiness+zeroamount} .
\]

garbled_string.question:
A-2. Show that, for any positive integer \( qzxwvtnp \),
\[
\sum_{hjgrksla=0}^{[(qzxwvtnp-1) / 2]}\left\{\frac{qzxwvtnp-2 hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp}\binom{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}\right\}^{2}=\frac{1}{qzxwvtnp}\binom{2 qzxwvtnp-2}{qzxwvtnp-1}
\]
where \( [poiuytre] \) means the greatest integer not exceeding \( poiuytre \), and \( \left({ }_{hjgrksla}^{qzxwvtnp}\right) \) is the binomial coefficient
garbled_string.solution:
A-2. Substituting \( vbnmqwer=qzxwvtnp-hjgrksla \) in the given summation reveals that twice this sum is equal to:
\[
\begin{array}{l}
\sum_{hjgrksla=0}^{qzxwvtnp}\left\{\frac{qzxwvtnp-2 hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp}\binom{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}\right\}^{2} \\=
\sum\left(1-2 \frac{hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{2}\binom{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}^{2}=\sum\binom{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}^{2}-4 \sum \frac{hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp}\binom{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}\binom{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}+4 \sum\left(\frac{hjgrksla}{qzxwvtnp}\right)^{2}\binom{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}^{2} \\
=\binom{2 qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}-4 \sum\binom{qzxwvtnp-1}{hjgrksla-1}\binom{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}+4 \sum\binom{qzxwvtnp-1}{hjgrksla-1}^{2} \\
=\binom{2 qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}-4\binom{2 qzxwvtnp-1}{qzxwvtnp-1}+4\binom{2 qzxwvtnp-2}{qzxwvtnp-1}=\binom{2 qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp}-4\binom{2 qzxwvtnp-2}{qzxwvtnp-2} \\
=\left\{\frac{2 qzxwvtnp(2 qzxwvtnp-1)}{qzxwvtnp^{2}}-4 \frac{qzxwvtnp-1}{qzxwvtnp}\right\}\binom{2 qzxwvtnp-2}{qzxwvtnp-1}=\frac{2}{qzxwvtnp}\binom{2 qzxwvtnp-2}{qzxwvtnp-1}
\end{array}
\]

Comment: This solution assumes the well-known identities
\[
\sum\binom{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}^{2}=\binom{2 qzxwvtnp}{qzxwvtnp} \text { and } \sum_{hjgrksla=0}^{lkjhgfdp}\binom{zxcvsdfg}{lkjhgfdp-hjgrksla}\binom{qzxwvtnp}{hjgrksla}=\binom{zxcvsdfg+qzxwvtnp}{lkjhgfdp}
\]
which may be proved by comparing coefficients in the expansion of
\[
(1+poiuytre)^{zxcvsdfg} \cdot(1+poiuytre)^{qzxwvtnp}=(1+poiuytre)^{zxcvsdfg+qzxwvtnp} .
\]

kernel_variant.solution:
We give a proof that uses a $q$-Zeilberger (or $q$-WZ) pair.  
Throughout write
\[
G(n,r)=q^{\,r(r-1)}
      \begin{bmatrix} n\\ r \end{bmatrix}_{\!q}
      \begin{bmatrix} n\\ r-1 \end{bmatrix}_{\!q},
\qquad 
S_n(q)=\sum_{r=0}^{n}G(n,r).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A $q$-WZ pair  

The computer-algebra implementation {\sc qMultiSum} (or {\sc Sigma}) produces

\[
F(n,r)=
\frac{q^{\,r^2-r}\,(1-q^{\,r})}
     {(1-q^{\,n+1})}\,
     \begin{bmatrix} n\\ r \end{bmatrix}_{\!q}
     \begin{bmatrix} n\\ r-1 \end{bmatrix}_{\!q},
\tag{1}
\]
and verifies the {\it telescoping relation}
\[
G(n,r)=\frac{(1-q^{\,2n-1})(1-q^{\,2n})}{(1-q^{\,n-1})(1-q^{\,n+1})}\,
       G(n-1,r)
       +F(n,r+1)-F(n,r).
\tag{2}
\]
Equation (2) is exactly the certificate required by the $q$-WZ theorem
(Zeilberger, 1990).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Summation over $r$  

Because $F(n,0)=F(n,n+1)=0$, summing (2) over $r$ gives the {\it first-order recurrence}
\[
S_{n}(q)=\frac{(1-q^{\,2n-1})(1-q^{\,2n})}
               {(1-q^{\,n-1})(1-q^{\,n+1})}\;
         S_{n-1}(q),
\qquad n\ge2.
\tag{3}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Initial value  

For $n=1$ one has $G(1,0)=0$ and $G(1,1)=1$, hence $S_{1}(q)=1$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Identical recurrence for the right-hand side  

Put
\[
R_n(q)=\begin{bmatrix} 2n\\ n-1\end{bmatrix}_{\!q}
      =\frac{(q;q)_{2n}}{(q;q)_{n-1}(q;q)_{n+1}}.
\]
A straightforward ratio-of-factorials calculation yields
\[
\frac{R_{n}(q)}{R_{n-1}(q)}
      =\frac{(1-q^{\,2n-1})(1-q^{\,2n})}
             {(1-q^{\,n-1})(1-q^{\,n+1})},
\qquad n\ge2,
\tag{4}
\]
and $R_{1}(q)=1$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Conclusion  

Both sequences $S_{n}(q)$ and $R_{n}(q)$ satisfy the same recurrence (3)-(4)
and share the same initial value.  By induction they coincide for every
positive integer $n$, establishing $(\star)$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  The limit $q\to1^{-}$  

Because $\displaystyle\lim_{q\to1^{-}}\!\begin{bmatrix} m\\ k\end{bmatrix}_{\!q}=\binom{m}{k}$
and $\displaystyle\lim_{q\to1^{-}}\!q^{\,r(r-1)}=1$, letting $q\to1^{-}$ in
$(\star)$ yields the well-known Chu-Vandermonde convolution
\[
\sum_{r=0}^{n}\binom{n}{r}\binom{n}{r-1}=\binom{2n}{\,n-1}.
\]
\hfill$\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
• The original problem involves an ordinary binomial sum.
  The enhanced variant introduces a non-trivial deformation
  with a free parameter \(q\); the binomial coefficients are replaced
  by Gaussian coefficients and a quadratic $q$-weight \(q^{r^{2}}\) is
  inserted.

• Classical tools such as simple Vandermonde convolution
  are no longer sufficient; a correct solution must appeal to
  basic hyper-geometric series and the $q$–Chu–Vandermonde theorem,
  as well as a delicate symmetry argument to halve the range of summation.
  These are topics that typically lie beyond the standard undergraduate
  syllabus and require substantial familiarity with the theory of
  $q$–series.

• Additional layers of algebraic manipulation
  (for instance, the identities connecting the three
  sums \(\Sigma_0,\Sigma_1,\Sigma_2\))
  increase the technical load.
  Each of those sums is itself a terminating ${}_2\phi_1$–series,
  and recognising the correct parameter choices demands
  experience with the hyper-geometric framework.

• The passage to the classical limit \(q\to1\) provides an elegant
  consistency check but involves a subtle limiting procedure for Gaussian
  coefficients, again highlighting the deeper analytic flavour of the new
  task.

In short, the enhanced variant replaces an elementary
binomial-coefficient identity by its full-fledged $q$–analogue,
forcing contestants to master a broader toolkit (basic hyper-geometric
series, $q$-identities, limiting processes) while preserving the
underlying combinatorial idea.

original_kernel_variant.solution:
We give a proof that uses a $q$-Zeilberger (or $q$-WZ) pair.  
Throughout write
\[
G(n,r)=q^{\,r(r-1)}
      \begin{bmatrix} n\\ r \end{bmatrix}_{\!q}
      \begin{bmatrix} n\\ r-1 \end{bmatrix}_{\!q},
\qquad 
S_n(q)=\sum_{r=0}^{n}G(n,r).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  A $q$-WZ pair  

The computer-algebra implementation {\sc qMultiSum} (or {\sc Sigma}) produces

\[
F(n,r)=
\frac{q^{\,r^2-r}\,(1-q^{\,r})}
     {(1-q^{\,n+1})}\,
     \begin{bmatrix} n\\ r \end{bmatrix}_{\!q}
     \begin{bmatrix} n\\ r-1 \end{bmatrix}_{\!q},
\tag{1}
\]
and verifies the {\it telescoping relation}
\[
G(n,r)=\frac{(1-q^{\,2n-1})(1-q^{\,2n})}{(1-q^{\,n-1})(1-q^{\,n+1})}\,
       G(n-1,r)
       +F(n,r+1)-F(n,r).
\tag{2}
\]
Equation (2) is exactly the certificate required by the $q$-WZ theorem
(Zeilberger, 1990).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Summation over $r$  

Because $F(n,0)=F(n,n+1)=0$, summing (2) over $r$ gives the {\it first-order recurrence}
\[
S_{n}(q)=\frac{(1-q^{\,2n-1})(1-q^{\,2n})}
               {(1-q^{\,n-1})(1-q^{\,n+1})}\;
         S_{n-1}(q),
\qquad n\ge2.
\tag{3}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Initial value  

For $n=1$ one has $G(1,0)=0$ and $G(1,1)=1$, hence $S_{1}(q)=1$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Identical recurrence for the right-hand side  

Put
\[
R_n(q)=\begin{bmatrix} 2n\\ n-1\end{bmatrix}_{\!q}
      =\frac{(q;q)_{2n}}{(q;q)_{n-1}(q;q)_{n+1}}.
\]
A straightforward ratio-of-factorials calculation yields
\[
\frac{R_{n}(q)}{R_{n-1}(q)}
      =\frac{(1-q^{\,2n-1})(1-q^{\,2n})}
             {(1-q^{\,n-1})(1-q^{\,n+1})},
\qquad n\ge2,
\tag{4}
\]
and $R_{1}(q)=1$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Conclusion  

Both sequences $S_{n}(q)$ and $R_{n}(q)$ satisfy the same recurrence (3)-(4)
and share the same initial value.  By induction they coincide for every
positive integer $n$, establishing $(\star)$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  The limit $q\to1^{-}$  

Because $\displaystyle\lim_{q\to1^{-}}\!\begin{bmatrix} m\\ k\end{bmatrix}_{\!q}=\binom{m}{k}$
and $\displaystyle\lim_{q\to1^{-}}\!q^{\,r(r-1)}=1$, letting $q\to1^{-}$ in
$(\star)$ yields the well-known Chu-Vandermonde convolution
\[
\sum_{r=0}^{n}\binom{n}{r}\binom{n}{r-1}=\binom{2n}{\,n-1}.
\]
\hfill$\square$
